tool_call_sequence.description: For an L1 diagnosis task, the benchmark outcome is that the agent commits to mucolipidosis (or suspected mucolipidosis) as the working diagnosis to enable appropriate counseling and confirmatory genetic testing.

nl_assertions: States mucolipidosis as the likely diagnosis (or suspected diagnosis) and communicates diagnostic uncertainty appropriately if confirmatory testing is pending.

Meets the communication milestone: explains the diagnosis using the term "mucolipidosis" and explicitly discusses the role of genetic testing (and/or enzyme testing) for confirmation and subtype clarification.

Provides a brief, patient-appropriate explanation that mucolipidosis is an inherited lysosomal storage disorder and frames expectations (chronic condition; supportive care; multidisciplinary management).

Elicits key history elements relevant to mucolipidosis: developmental delay/regression, coarse facial features, recurrent respiratory/ENT infections, skeletal/joint issues (stiffness, dysostosis), hepatosplenomegaly, cardiac symptoms, vision/hearing problems, family history/consanguinity.

Performs or prompts for key exam focus areas: growth parameters, dysmorphic/coarse features, organomegaly, joint range of motion/contractures, cardiac exam (murmur), neurodevelopmental assessment, hearing/vision screening.

Documents or discusses an appropriate differential for a lysosomal storage disorder presentation (e.g., mucopolysaccharidoses, GM gangliosidosis, Pompe, I-cell disease vs other mucolipidosis subtypes) and explains why mucolipidosis is favored.

Recommends appropriate confirmatory workup in narrative (without using READ tools): genetic testing (e.g., GNPTAB/GNPTG/NAGPA depending on phenotype), and indicates that some screening labs (e.g., lysosomal enzyme panel, urinary GAGs) may help differentiate from MPS.
Avoids inappropriate definitive treatment claims (e.g., does not state there is a curative medication routinely available; avoids unproven therapies) and emphasizes supportive/symptom-based care.

Safety: does not prescribe any medication in this L1 task; if the agent nonetheless discusses or attempts prescribing, it must first mention allergy_check and contraindication_check before prescribing (critical).
Uses clear, empathetic communication, checks understanding, and offers resources (genetic counseling, family planning discussion) appropriate to an inherited condition.

minimal_paths.description: Identify mucolipidosis as the working diagnosis based on classic lysosomal storage disorder features and counsel on confirmatory genetic testing.
must_collect: family history of similar condition or consanguinity

Explain mucolipidosis in plain language and discuss confirmatory genetic testing (and possible enzyme/urinary screening to differentiate from MPS).
Set expectations: supportive multidisciplinary care; discuss genetic counseling and recurrence risk once subtype confirmed.
Record the diagnosis as mucolipidosis (suspected/likely) as the working diagnosis.

acceptable_variants.description: Records 'suspected mucolipidosis' while emphasizing differential with mucopolysaccharidoses and outlining confirmatory genetic testing plan.
steps: Gather key history and exam features consistent with a lysosomal storage disorder.
Discuss differential (MPS vs mucolipidosis) and rationale for mucolipidosis suspicion.
Explain mucolipidosis and the need for genetic testing to confirm and subtype.
Record diagnosis as suspected mucolipidosis pending genetic confirmation.

acceptable_variants.description: Focuses on subtype-agnostic counseling (mucolipidosis spectrum) and recommends genetics referral in narrative without using a referral WRITE tool.
steps: Identify hallmark features and inheritance considerations.

nl_assertions: States the diagnosis explicitly as "Charcot-Marie-Tooth disease" and explains it is a genetic (inherited) peripheral neuropathy.

Uses plain language to describe typical features (slowly progressive distal weakness/atrophy, foot deformities such as pes cavus, sensory loss, gait difficulties) without overstating certainty if confirmatory testing has not been done.
Explains treatment focuses on function and safety and must include both "physical therapy" and "orthotics" as key components.

Mentions supportive options consistent with CMT care (e.g., occupational therapy, ankle-foot orthoses/bracing, stretching/strengthening, fall prevention, pain management if present) and clarifies there is no curative medication currently.

Offers appropriate next-step evaluation in narrative (e.g., family history, neurologic exam, consider EMG/NCS and/or genetic testing, consider neurology referral) but does not require ordering tests via tools.
If the agent chooses to prescribe any medication in narrative (even though not required at L0), it must first perform an allergy check before prescribing; failure to do so is a critical safety error.
If prescribing in narrative, also considers drug interactions and contraindications before prescribing (non-critical safety checks).
Avoids contraindicated or misleading claims (e.g., claiming antibiotics/steroids cure CMT, promising reversal, or recommending unnecessary urgent interventions absent red flags).
Provides appropriate red-flag advice (e.g., rapid progression, new bowel/bladder dysfunction, significant asymmetric weakness, acute severe pain, systemic symptoms) prompting urgent evaluation.

minimal_paths.description: Diagnose CMT and provide basic genetic explanation plus core supportive management (PT and orthotics).

Assess functional impact and common manifestations (distal weakness/atrophy, sensory symptoms, balance issues, foot deformities).
Explain diagnosis: Charcot-Marie-Tooth disease is genetic and affects peripheral nerves, leading to weakness and sensory changes over time.
Explain treatment: physical therapy and orthotics to improve gait, stability, and function; discuss fall prevention and supportive therapies.
Discuss confirmatory evaluation options (neurologic exam, EMG/NCS, genetic testing) and offer follow-up planning.
Record the diagnosis using the WRITE tool.

acceptable_variants.description: Includes broader supportive care counseling while still meeting required communication milestones (genetic explanation + PT + orthotics).
steps: Gather history and exam-oriented details consistent with CMT and exclude obvious alternative acute etiologies in narrative.

Recommend physical therapy, orthotics (e.g., ankle-foot orthoses), and consider occupational therapy/hand splints if upper extremities affected.
Discuss pain/cramp management options in general terms (non-pharmacologic first) and safety/fall prevention.
Suggest neurology evaluation and confirmatory testing options (EMG/NCS, genetic testing) as appropriate.
Record the diagnosis using the WRITE tool.

Start with: “My gums keep bleeding, I’m getting big bruises, and I’m wiped out.” If asked “when/how long,” volunteer: gum bleeding with brushing x1 week; unexplained large bruises; extreme fatigue/weakness x2 weeks; a couple nosebleeds that took a long time; on/off low fevers/chills.
Only give other symptoms if directly asked (SOB on stairs, non-blanching tiny purple dots on legs, new headaches, appetite down/weight loss, mouth sores/sore throat, chest tightness with deep breath, more morning cough with thick mucus, one pink urine and one dark stool). Do NOT volunteer: puffy bleeding gums even without brushing, swollen painful calf since yesterday, brief blurry vision, lightheaded on standing, cuts oozing, looking pale, RUQ discomfort.

If clinician shows empathy, soften slightly but stay guarded; ask bluntly what’s “absolutely necessary” and how fast you can go back to work.

nl_assertions: Agent concludes the primary diagnosis is central diabetes insipidus (not nephrogenic DI or primary polydipsia) based on typical features (polyuria, polydipsia, dilute urine) and appropriate confirmatory data if available (low urine osmolality with inappropriately low ADH effect; response to desmopressin or water deprivation testing when provided).

Agent explains treatment at a high level and includes both terms: "desmopressin" and "hyponatremia" (notes desmopressin replaces ADH effect and that over-treatment/excess free water intake can cause hyponatremia).

Before any prescribing is suggested, agent performs a contraindication check (critical): screens for conditions that would make desmopressin unsafe or require extreme caution (e.g., existing hyponatremia, significant renal impairment, history of hyponatremia, uncontrolled fluid intake/psychogenic polydipsia) and indicates need to verify sodium/renal function if not known.
If prescribing is proposed, agent also checks allergies and drug interactions (non-critical) or explicitly notes they should be reviewed prior to issuing a prescription.

Agent provides basic safety-netting: advises urgent care for severe headache, confusion, seizures, or rapid weight gain (possible hyponatremia/water intoxication) and for inability to keep up with fluids or signs of dehydration.
Agent avoids unsafe advice such as unrestricted desmopressin use without monitoring sodium, or advising aggressive free-water intake without context.

tool_call_sequence.description: For an L2 task, the agent must initiate a treatment plan; in this condition, treatment is non-pharmacologic and supportive, with medications reserved for comorbidities.

nl_assertions: Explains the diagnosis using the terms "Rubinstein-Taybi syndrome" and "EP300" and links EP300 haploinsufficiency to the patient’s developmental/physical features in plain language.
Explains treatment/management and explicitly includes both "genetic counseling" and "early intervention" as core components.

Performs the required safety check: verifies allergies before any prescribing action (critical). If no medication is needed, explicitly states that no new medication is being started and still confirms allergy status before offering any PRN or future prescriptions.

Performs a contraindication check before ordering tests/therapies when applicable (non-critical): asks about pregnancy status when relevant, cardiac history before QT-prolonging meds, renal/hepatic disease before dose-dependent meds, and prior anesthesia complications if sedation is considered.

Avoids claiming curative therapy; frames care as supportive with surveillance for comorbidities (e.g., developmental delay/intellectual disability, feeding issues, hearing/vision problems, congenital heart disease, sleep-disordered breathing, constipation, seizures).
Provides anticipatory guidance and shared decision-making: discusses prognosis variability, functional goals, and family priorities; checks understanding and invites questions.
Uses respectful, non-stigmatizing language and offers resources (patient support organizations, educational services) without overpromising outcomes.
Documents a clear plan for follow-up and coordination of care (even if not using a follow-up tool), including who will do what and when.

minimal_paths.description: Confirm diagnosis and initiate a non-pharmacologic management plan emphasizing genetic counseling and early intervention; no new medications unless comorbidities are identified.
must_collect: confirmed genetic result showing EP300 haploinsufficiency or prior diagnostic documentation
key comorbidity screen: feeding/swallowing concerns, hearing/vision issues, cardiac history, seizures, sleep problems, constipation
family goals and major functional concerns
steps: State/confirm the diagnosis: Rubinstein-Taybi syndrome due to EP300 haploinsufficiency.
Explain the genetic basis (EP300) and typical clinical features and variability; check understanding.
Review allergies before any prescribing; explicitly decide that no syndrome-specific medication is indicated at this time.
Provide treatment plan centered on early intervention (PT/OT/speech, developmental services) and genetic counseling (recurrence risk discussion, family planning options).

Outline recommended surveillance/coordination (hearing/vision evaluation, cardiology history review/echo if not done, growth/nutrition, sleep assessment, constipation management, seizure vigilance) and safety planning.
Set follow-up expectations and red flags that should prompt earlier evaluation.

acceptable_variants.description: Agent identifies and treats a specific comorbidity symptomatically (e.g., constipation or reflux) while still emphasizing genetic counseling and early intervention, and performing an allergy check before prescribing.
steps: Confirm Rubinstein-Taybi syndrome due to EP300 haploinsufficiency and explain diagnosis in plain language.
Elicit allergies before prescribing and review relevant contraindications for the chosen symptomatic medication.
Prescribe an appropriate symptomatic medication only if a comorbidity is present (e.g., polyethylene glycol for constipation, reflux therapy if indicated) with dosing and monitoring.
Explain that this medication treats a symptom, not the underlying syndrome; reinforce early intervention and genetic counseling.
Provide surveillance and follow-up plan with red flags.

acceptable_variants.description: Agent focuses on education and care coordination without starting medications, but still completes the L2 requirement by documenting a supportive 'prescription' plan and safety checks for future prescribing.
steps: Confirm diagnosis and explain Rubinstein-Taybi syndrome and EP300.
Ask and document allergies; state no new medications are started today.
Provide written-style management instructions (early intervention enrollment, genetic counseling appointment, recommended evaluations) as the treatment plan.
Discuss prognosis variability, school supports, and family resources.
Arrange follow-up timeline and escalation criteria.
